title: Will Peru’s booming economy survive its latest election?
gist: Peru's 7 June runoff between conservative Keiko Fujimori and leftist outsider Roberto Sánchez threatens to break the long-standing decoupling between Peru's political chaos (eight presidents in 10 years) and its strong average 5.5% GDP growth, with Sánchez's state-interventionist, mining-tax platform worrying investors.

insights.claim: Despite eight presidents in the past 10 years, Peru's economy grew at an average 5.5 per cent annually between 2002 and 2022, excluding a 10 per cent COVID-19 contraction, showing political turmoil and economic growth have historically coexisted.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The historical decoupling of politics and growth is the baseline investors have relied on; the article's thesis is that this year's election could be the moment that decoupling ends.
insights.locator: It's commonly noted that Peru has had eight presidents

insights.claim: The 7 June 2026 runoff between Keiko Fujimori and Roberto Sánchez may mark the moment the balance between political crisis and economic growth finally breaks in Peru.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Investors in Peruvian assets (mining equities, sovereign bonds, sol) should treat this election as a genuine regime-risk event rather than a repeat of prior cliffhangers that left growth intact.

themes.theme: Sánchez's interventionist platform and investor risk

insights.claim: Roberto Sánchez is campaigning on greater state intervention in the economy including in Peru's mining sector, which accounts for 9.5 per cent of GDP and has driven the country's growth over the past decade and a half.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A Sánchez win puts the mining sector's fiscal and regulatory regime directly at risk, a key variable for foreign mining investors and Peru-exposed commodity supply chains (copper).
insights.locator: He is also running on greater state intervention

insights.claim: Sánchez has pledged to raise taxes on the wealthy and use international reserves to boost government spending, promises that have worried investors and the local business community.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A tax-and-spend platform funded by drawing down reserves signals potential currency and fiscal-stability risk if Sánchez wins and implements it.
insights.locator: Those promises, and a pledge to raise taxes

insights.claim: Sánchez has called for a constituent assembly to rewrite Peru's constitution and has adopted the political symbolism (a white sombrero) of former president Pedro Castillo.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A push to rewrite the constitution signals potential for deeper institutional and property-rights uncertainty beyond a single presidential term, relevant to long-horizon capital allocation decisions.
insights.locator: The surprise first-round winner has taken

themes.theme: Structural drivers of Peru's political instability

insights.claim: Alberto Fujimori's 1992-2000 rule as effective dictator caused Peru's party system to evaporate, and every presidential election since 2000 has gone to a cliffhanger second round with wildly swinging voter preferences.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The fragmented, personality-driven party system is a structural (not cyclical) source of Peru political risk that will likely persist regardless of who wins this election.
insights.locator: During his presidency Peru's party system evaporated

insights.claim: Congressional impeachment and removal of presidents has become a political tradition in Peru since Toledo, with the last president to complete a full term being Ollanta Humala in 2016 (who is now imprisoned for corruption).
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The base rate for any incoming Peruvian president to survive a full term is low, implying policy continuity risk regardless of the election outcome.

insights.claim: A 2024 constitutional reform recreated a two-chamber legislature (adding a Senate) and imposed a vote-share floor for parties, reducing the number of parties in the lower Chamber of Deputies to six after the April 2026 elections, down from a fractured 12-party unicameral body.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Institutional reform toward fewer, larger parties could reduce legislative gridlock risk over time, a mildly positive structural signal for governability even amid the current volatile election.
insights.locator: But the constitution was reformed in 2024

insights.claim: Keiko Fujimori's Fuerza Popular party, the largest plurality in the legislature, has repeatedly used corruption allegations as a pretext to hamstring and remove non-Fuerza-Popular presidents, casting doubt on the sincerity of her anti-corruption platform.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A Keiko win would not necessarily bring political stability despite her 'macro-economic stability' platform, since her party has itself been a driver of past instability via aggressive impeachment tactics.
insights.locator: But others were just a pretext to hamstring

insights.claim: In the April 2026 first round, Rafael López Aliaga, the tough-on-crime Trump-adjacent former mayor of Lima who was widely expected to advance, lost to Sánchez by just 21,000 votes after logistical problems extended the elections, and he contested the results.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: López Aliaga's contested loss and potential to rally opposition support against a Sánchez win is a latent source of post-election unrest and legitimacy disputes to monitor.
insights.locator: But in a surprise and contentious finish

insights.claim: Sánchez is an ally of former president Pedro Castillo, who was removed from office after attempting to impose a state of emergency in 2022 and was sentenced to 12 years in prison for rebellion and conspiracy against the state; Castillo drew support from rural, indigenous, extractive-economy-frontline communities underrepresented in national politics.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sánchez's coalition draws on the same rural/indigenous base that backed Castillo's failed self-coup, suggesting his presidency could face similar confrontations with Congress and similar risk of institutional crisis.
insights.locator: An exception was the case of Pedro Castillo
